stem: A hospital uses a CT scan screening to screen for early-stage lung cancer. The test has a sensitivity of 99% and a specificity of 83%. The prevalence of early-stage lung cancer in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.0% and 5.6%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has early-stage lung cancer? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(early-stage lung cancer | positive test) ranges from 0.106 to 0.257 as prevalence varies from 2.0% to 5.6%. Point estimate at 3.8% prevalence: 0.187.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.17.
The confidence interval [0.106, 0.257] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (99%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
